profile.statement_to_voters:
I am Stephen Andrews, and I am standing as your local Green Party candidate for Spital Ward.

This by-election comes at a crucial time for Tamworth; we are about to see the largest change in how our council is run and how services are delivered in a generation. At a moment like this, Spital needs a strong, local voice that listens to residents, stands up for the community, and puts people first.

For too long, residents have been let down by the two-party system. The Conservatives and Labour have both failed to deliver for Tamworth. Reform are no different: they have no policies for Spital and no plan for Tamworth. I believe that we deserve better. We deserve honest representation that puts Tamworth first and prioritises our future.

I look at Spital and the failure of the past few years is evident:
•	You see the pain that leaseholders have on your everyday lives. 
•	You see the failure of the Government to hold water companies to account for pollution in our rivers and crumbling infrastructure, leading to floods that we’re all too familiar with.
•	You see the potential negative implications of new developments being built.

Instead, I want to be the voice to work towards change:
•	I will advocate for the end of the leasehold system; and work with residents who are struggling with getting essential repairs done.
•	I will put pressure on the water companies, making sure they are held accountable.
•	I will research, question, and challenge all new developments, ensuring the town’s best interests are at heart.

I am ready to put in the work to ensure that every voice is heard; together, we can make this happen.

I am a hard working family man that was born in Tamworth and live in the Watling division. I am standing for Reform Uk as for to long have Labour and the conservative parties failed to deliver on the promises they make while increasing taxes and delivering less services. The time is now for change not the same old story of failed policies.

profile.statement_to_voters:
Dear resident,

When I said I would only stand in the Stanford Ward by-election if local people thought I should, I meant it.

So I want to thank all of the people who took the time to let me know their thoughts in response to my recent letter.

I’ve read every comment and I was very moved that 97% of people said I should stand in the election - including people who had never voted for my party before, but recognised that I don’t make being a Councillor about party politics, but about working hard and getting things done for our area.

Having listened to you, I’ve made up my mind: I will be standing to represent our area on the District Council.

In this by-election, my message is simple. In the 9 months I’ve been your County Councillor, I’ve worked hard to get things done. That’s what I’ll do as your District Councillor, too.

My record as a County Councillor includes:

- Getting the A417 by Stanford Mill and the Shellingford crossroads into the Council’s plans for resurfacing. Local roads have been neglected by the County Council for years. I spoke about the state of the A417 at the first opportunity in Full Council, brought Council officers to our area to inspect the road for themselves, and kept on fighting until the Council agreed to resurface the road. Work is beginning this month.

- Reporting potholes, blocked drains and damaged pavements directly to the Council - and demanding action for our area. When I first got elected, it became obvious to me that Council systems for identifying and repairing potholes, blocked drains and damaged pavements aren’t working. So I’ve been going out and reporting issues myself - and staying on the case until the job is done.

- Speaking up for our area in the Council chamber. I have a 100% attendance record as a Councillor and never miss the chance to speak up for our area and local residents. For example, when the Lib Dems (who run the Council) scrapped the dedicated Cabinet Member for Special Educational Needs & Disabilities, I spoke - and voted - against it.

- Opposing policies that make life harder. When the County Council announced unnecessary new rules for using the tip, I challenged them. In meeting after meeting I asked about the risk of more fly-tipping, of Oxfordshire residents being charged, and queues forming at the Stanford tip because of new paperwork checks. I will continue to fight this unnecessary policy.

Since being elected as a County Councillor, many people have approached me about District Council issues.

As I’m not currently on the District Council, it’s been frustrating that I haven’t been able to do more to help. But I’ve jotted down the issues people raised with me and they will be my priorities if you elect me onto the District Council. They include:

- Protecting the countryside - by opposing inappropriate development, especially when it doesn’t come with the investment in transport and facilities our villages need.

- Tidying up public spaces - like so many of you, I want to see a renewed focus on cleaning pavements and tackling fly-tipping, so we never feel ashamed of the condition of our area.

- Fixing our roads - although roads are a County Council matter, some of the District Council’s work is relevant (like planning). I’ll use my voice on the District Council to continue my fight to fix our roads.

- Preserving fortnightly black bin collection - which is at risk because of the Lib Dems’ plan to merge our Council with West Berkshire, where it has gone to every three weeks. If I’m your Councillor, I would oppose these changes.

In the weeks ahead, I’ll be out speaking to residents and setting out my plan to stand up for our area on the District Council. I hope to speak to you soon.

In the meantime, if there is anything I can do to help, please don’t hesitate to get in touch.

All best wishes,

Cllr Lee Evans
Local Conservative candidate for the Stanford Ward by-election

profile.statement_to_voters:
Oliver Leach: Proven Service, A Local Voice for Haslemere

​Dear Neighbour,

​Haslemere is more than just where I live—it is the community I serve every day. As your candidate for the West Surrey Unitary Council, I am running to ensure that our town’s unique needs are at the forefront of every decision. My platform is simple: Haslemere First.

​A Record of Listening and Action

​I believe a councillor should be someone who truly understands the heartbeat of their town. My experience in Haslemere is rooted in local action and genuine service:

​Local Business, Local Heart: As the owner of Oliver’s, I see the challenges our high street and our residents face firsthand. My shop is a hub for conversation, and I am proud to support our community by providing free hot meals once a month at the Haslemere Food Bank. I don’t just talk about community spirit; I live it. ​The Power of Listening: Since 2011, I have served as a Guildford Samaritan, including three years as Branch Director (2016–2019). This role taught me that the most important skill a representative can have is the ability to listen—really listen—to people. Listening is my strength, and it is exactly what I will bring to the council. ​A Champion for West Surrey: I will bring that same dedication to the Council. Whether it is advocating for better local infrastructure, protecting our green spaces, or supporting our local families, I will be a calm, determined voice for you. ​My Promises to You

​If elected, I will use my experience as a business owner and a volunteer to:

​Be Your Voice: I am trained to listen and equipped to lead. I will be your direct line to the Unitary Council, ensuring Haslemere’s concerns are heard loud and clear. ​Support the Vulnerable: Using my background in service to ensure that community support and local welfare remain a priority in our region. ​Protect Our Town’s Character: Ensuring that our growth is sustainable and that we preserve what makes Haslemere a special place to live. ​Put the Town First on Election Day

​I am not looking for a title; I am looking to work for you. I have spent years listening to the people of this area, and now I want to take what I’ve heard and turn it into action at the council level.

​On election day, vote for a candidate who knows our streets, supports our neighbours, and has a proven track record of putting the community first.

​Vote Oliver Leach.

I am the Conservative Party candidate for the upcoming Haslemere Town, Waverley Borough, and Surrey County Council by-elections in our great town of Haslemere.

My family have called Haslemere 'home' for generations, and I want to give back to the town that has done so much for us by serving residents in Haslemere and the surrounding villages. As someone with business experience, I will bring economic competence and sound judgement to all of these councils, and will be a visible and active presence across Haslemere.

Alongside running monthly surgeries with residents and launching a new 'pothole patrol' to push for road repairs in our town to be prioritised, I will also campaign against the problematic plan put forward by the Liberal Democrats to develop on the Wey Hill car park site. I will also push for a fairer deal for so many commuters who are forced to pay eye-watering sums just to travel to work.

I am the Operations Director at a tech startup in London that I've helped build for the last 8 years, and a listening volunteer at Samaritans in Guildford. I am also the proud owner of a Countdown teapot, having won 8 games in a row on Channel 4's Countdown in 2024 (granting me the title of 'Octochamp'!).

Feel free to get in touch with me at toby4haslemere@gmail.com. And make sure to vote Conservative on July the 7th!

I have lived in Haslemere since 2009. Having grown up in a small town with experience in a family-owned business, I believe hard working, law-abiding taxpayers are the back  bone of the nation.

It has been of  great concern to observe the decline of the UK under previous and current governments, having abandoned British citizens by failing to deliver on their promises and to carry out the will of the people.

Some of the concerns that are affecting British citizens on a national and local level are:
* open borders and agenda to eliminate sovereign nations
* lawlessness and injustice in the judicial system
* loss of freedom of speech and religious expression (most notably Christian), loss 
  of parental authority, breakdown in the family unit.
* an absence of Christian principles which has underpinned the UK and other free 
 societies for centuries.
* decline in merit-based advancement, high taxation and government overreach.
* association with the European Court of Human Rights and WHO

These are just a few of the globalist/Marxist/secular humanist policies, along with social immorality which have contributed the the UK's decline--spiritually, economically, morally and militarily.

As a Christian, I believe in the return to long-held Christian values, which are pivotal in achieving liberty, prosperity and justice for all people.

I believe the Reformation of this country must begin at the local, grassroots level and this is why I am standing for West Surrey Council representing Haslemere.

These issues require boldness to promote truth, love for nation and to put the best interests of UK citizens and taxpayers first. REFORM UK is the only party with the bold, decisive leadership necessary to deliver this change in direction.

I will:
* Promote the preservation of Greenbelt land, local character, British culture and patriotism.
* Repair roads and update utilities in a timely manner--minimising disruption to local traffic.
* Challenge wasteful policies, releasing money into increasing local services.
* Support parental rights, while opposing destructive, woke ideology in the culture.

I will listen to your concerns and work on your behalf to produce an environment that is conducive to achieving a better quality of life for you, your children and grandchildren.

I will support and promote common sense policies that put the residents of Haslemere first, where decisions are made locally and stand against cronyism, government overreach and policies that subvert the public good.

**About me**

I'm Tom Shrive, your local Green Party candidate for the Haslemere triple by-election on 7th July. I live and work in Haslemere with my wife and young family, and I chose to raise my children here because it's such a special place.

I studied sustainable economics and run a technology business. I know how to manage budgets alongside social and environmental challenges, and I want to bring that experience to local government.

**My priorities**

When I speak with residents, the same priorities come up again and again:

- Protecting local services
- Ensuring council money is spent wisely
- Safeguarding the green spaces that make Haslemere what it is

If elected, I will fight to make sure any new development also invests in roads, community healthcare, schools and local facilities.

**What I will push for**

- A clear public tracker so residents can see where development money goes and what it delivers
- The proposed step-free bridge linking the Leisure Centre and Wey Hill to Tesco, making it easier to walk or cycle across town instead of driving
- Haslemere Town Council keeping control of local facilities, with proper budgets and full transparency over spending

I believe Haslemere's parks, woodlands and natural spaces must be protected for future generations, and that affordable housing for local families, young people and key workers should be delivered in the right places with the right infrastructure.

**Staying in touch**

I will hold ward surgeries both in person and online, so residents always have a way to raise local issues with me directly.

I am Hazel EDWARDS former mayor of Barrow and councillor of many years. I am the REFORM UK candidate for Hawcoat and Newbarns ward (Westmorland and Furness Council) & Hawcoat Ward (Barrow Town Council) in the double by-election on 4th June. I am standing for both councils.

I live in the ward. I know our community and its challenges.

Barrow is an important cog in the national machine and I have seen it forgotten by Westmorland and Furness council.

I am returning to politics to be a strong voice with others for our town and, hopefully, improve life for families, businesses and residents.

REFORM UK is the way forward. We have huge support in the town. Please vote REFORM on 4th June.

I’m standing as your Labour candidate for Hawcoat and Newbarns in the Westmorland and Furness Council by-election next month.

I’ve been living in this ward for over four decades, and I’m a Barrow Town Councillor for Newbarns.

I’m retired now and have the time to dedicate to working with the council to make sure our services run better. From fixing potholes to making our streets safer for both pedestrians and drivers, I will hold the Lib Dem run council to account.

What it means to me is being someone who is constantly there, a listening ear for the people of Hawcoat and Newbarns, with a voice who can then transform those concerns and those worries and those voices into something tangible at council level, holding, whether it’s Labour at Barrow Town Council or the Liberal Democrats at Westmorland and Furness Council, to account.

With our four years of Conservative representation of Hawcoat and Newbarns, we’ve done wonderful things, but yet still we find ourselves completely neglected by the Liberal Democrat majority on Westmorland and Furness Council.

I think the problems facing Hawcoat and Newbarns are ubiquitous, they are facing everyone in Westmorland and Furness, whether it’s poor road networks, lack of communication with businesses, we have recently seen the forced purchase of businesses at Lakes Parade.

We have seen public facilities such as Hawcoat Community Centre being left to the winds of time, with Westmorland and Furness Council focusing all of its might and power on Kendal and neglecting our small patch down here.

Really, my roots to the area couldn’t grow any deeper, my family has been here in Cumbria and in Lancashire for hundreds and hundreds of years. I feel intrinsically linked to the area.

I imagine that the criticism that comes from my candidacy will be synonymous with inexperience, but I would like to take issue with that contention.

As it was reinforced to me by residents on the doorstep, it is a refreshing opportunity for local politics, in a sense that you’re bringing in, not an external voice, but a refreshing voice with new perspectives and new abilities to speak to people in a way that perhaps they haven’t experienced before.
